  to correctly estimate the GW distance.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

solver: string, optional

: Solver to use either 'PGD' for Projected Gradient Descent or 'PPA'
  for Proximal Point Algorithm.
  Default value is 'PGD'.

warmstart: bool, optional

: Either to perform warmstart of dual potentials in the successive
  Sinkhorn projections.

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: Record log if True.

kwargs: dict

: parameters can be directly passed to the ot.sinkhorn solver.
  Such as `numItermax` and `stopThr` to control its estimation precision,
  e.g \[51\] suggests to use `numItermax=1`.

# Returns

gw_dist

: Gromov-Wasserstein distance

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gabriel Peyré, Marco Cuturi, and Justin Solomon,
    "Gromov-Wasserstein averaging of kernel and distance matrices."
    International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML). 2016.

[^footnote-2]: Xu, H., Luo, D., Zha, H., & Duke, L. C. (2019). Gromov-wasserstein
    learning for graph matching and node embedding. In International
    Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2019.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.BAPG_gromov_wasserstein
documentation:
Returns the Gromov-Wasserstein transport between $(\mathbf{C_1}, \mathbf{p})$ and $(\mathbf{C_2}, \mathbf{q})$
estimated using Bregman Alternated Projected Gradient method.

If `marginal_loss=True`, the function solves the following Gromov-Wasserstein
optimization problem :

$$
\mathbf{T}^* \in \mathop{\arg\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l}

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Else, the function solves an equivalent problem \[63\], where constant terms only
depending on the marginals $\mathbf{p}$: and $\mathbf{q}$: are
discarded while assuming that L decomposes as in Proposition 1 in \[12\]:

$$
\mathbf{T}^* \in\mathop{\arg\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad - \langle h_1(\mathbf{C}_1) \mathbf{T} h_2(\mathbf{C_2})^\top , \mathbf{T} \rangle_F

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{C_1}$: Metric cost matrix in the source space
- $\mathbf{C_2}$: Metric cost matrix in the target space
- $\mathbf{p}$: distribution in the source space
- $\mathbf{q}$: distribution in the target space
- `L`: loss function to account for the misfit between the similarity matrices
  : satisfying $L(a, b) = f_1(a) + f_2(b) - h_1(a) h_2(b)$

:::{note}
By algorithmic design the optimal coupling $\mathbf{T}$
returned by this function does not necessarily satisfy the marginal
constraints $\mathbf{T}\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{p}$ and
$\mathbf{T}^T\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{q}$. So the returned
Gromov-Wasserstein loss does not necessarily satisfy distance
properties and may be negative.
:::

# Parameters

C1

: Metric cost matrix in the source space

C2

: Metric cost matrix in the target space

p

: Distribution in the source space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

q

: Distribution in the target space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either C1 and C2 are to be assumed symmetric or not.
  If let to its default None value, a symmetry test will be conducted.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

G0: array-like, shape (ns,nt), optional

: If None the initial transport plan of the solver is pq^T.
  Otherwise G0 will be used as initial transport of the solver. G0 is not
  required to satisfy marginal constraints but we strongly recommend it
  to correctly estimate the GW distance.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

marginal_loss: bool, optional. Default is False.

: Include constant marginal terms or not in the objective function.

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: Record log if True.

# Returns

T

: Optimal coupling between the two spaces

# References

[^footnote-1]: Li, J., Tang, J., Kong, L., Liu, H., Li, J., So, A. M. C., & Blanchet, J.
    "A Convergent Single-Loop Algorithm for Relaxation of Gromov-Wasserstein
    in Graph Data". International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2022.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.BAPG_gromov_wasserstein2
documentation:
Returns the Gromov-Wasserstein loss $\mathbf{GW}$ between $(\mathbf{C_1}, \mathbf{p})$ and $(\mathbf{C_2}, \mathbf{q})$
estimated using Bregman Alternated Projected Gradient method.

If `marginal_loss=True`, the function solves the following Gromov-Wasserstein
optimization problem :

$$
\mathbf{GW} = \mathop{\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l}

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Else, the function solves an equivalent problem \[63, 64\], where constant terms only
depending on the marginals $\mathbf{p}$: and $\mathbf{q}$: are
discarded while assuming that L decomposes as in Proposition 1 in \[12\]:

$$
\mathop{\min}_\mathbf{T}  \quad - \langle h_1(\mathbf{C}_1) \mathbf{T} h_2(\mathbf{C_2})^\top , \mathbf{T} \rangle_F

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{C_1}$: Metric cost matrix in the source space
- $\mathbf{C_2}$: Metric cost matrix in the target space
- $\mathbf{p}$: distribution in the source space
- $\mathbf{q}$: distribution in the target space
- `L`: loss function to account for the misfit between the similarity matrices
  : satisfying $L(a, b) = f_1(a) + f_2(b) - h_1(a) h_2(b)$

:::{note}
By algorithmic design the optimal coupling $\mathbf{T}$
returned by this function does not necessarily satisfy the marginal
constraints $\mathbf{T}\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{p}$ and
$\mathbf{T}^T\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{q}$. So the returned
Gromov-Wasserstein loss does not necessarily satisfy distance
properties and may be negative.
:::

# Parameters

C1

: Metric cost matrix in the source space

C2

: Metric cost matrix in the target space

p

: Distribution in the source space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

q

: Distribution in the target space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either C1 and C2 are to be assumed symmetric or not.
  If let to its default None value, a symmetry test will be conducted.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

G0: array-like, shape (ns,nt), optional

: If None the initial transport plan of the solver is pq^T.
  Otherwise G0 will be used as initial transport of the solver. G0 is not
  required to satisfy marginal constraints but we strongly recommend it
  to correctly estimate the GW distance.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

marginal_loss: bool, optional. Default is False.

: Include constant marginal terms or not in the objective function.

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: Record log if True.

# Returns

gw_dist

: Gromov-Wasserstein distance

# References

[^footnote-1]: Li, J., Tang, J., Kong, L., Liu, H., Li, J., So, A. M. C., & Blanchet, J.
    "A Convergent Single-Loop Algorithm for Relaxation of Gromov-Wasserstein
    in Graph Data". International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2023.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.entropic_gromov_barycenters
documentation:
Returns the Gromov-Wasserstein barycenters of `S` measured similarity matrices $(\mathbf{C}_s)_{1 \leq s \leq S}$
estimated using Gromov-Wasserstein transports from Sinkhorn projections.

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\mathbf{C}^* = \mathop{\arg \min}_{\mathbf{C}\in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}} \quad \sum_s \lambda_s \mathrm{GW}(\mathbf{C}, \mathbf{C}_s, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{p}_s)
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{C}_s$: metric cost matrix
- $\mathbf{p}_s$: distribution

# Parameters

N

: Size of the targeted barycenter

Cs

: Metric cost matrices

ps

: Sample weights in the `S` spaces.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distributions are taken.

p

: Weights in the targeted barycenter.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

lambdas

: List of the `S` spaces' weights.
  If let to its default value None, uniform weights are taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either structures are to be assumed symmetric or not. Default value is True.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

stop_criterion

: Convergence criterion taking values in \['barycenter', 'loss'\]. If set to 'barycenter'
  uses absolute norm variations of estimated barycenters. Else if set to 'loss'
  uses the relative variations of the loss.

warmstartT: bool, optional

: Either to perform warmstart of transport plans in the successive
  gromov-wasserstein transport problems.

verbose

: Print information along iterations.

log

: Record log if True.

init_C

: Random initial value for the $\mathbf{C}$ matrix provided by user.

random_state

: Fix the seed for reproducibility

kwargs: dict

: parameters can be directly passed to the `ot.entropic_gromov_wasserstein` solver.

# Returns

C

: Similarity matrix in the barycenter space (permutated arbitrarily)

log

: Only returned when log=True. It contains the keys:

  - $\mathbf{T}$: list of (`N`, `ns`) transport matrices
  - $\mathbf{p}$: (`N`,) barycenter weights
  - values used in convergence evaluation.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gabriel Peyré, Marco Cuturi, and Justin Solomon,
    "Gromov-Wasserstein averaging of kernel and distance matrices."
    International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML). 2016.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.entropic_fused_gromov_wasserstein
documentation:
Returns the Fused Gromov-Wasserstein transport between $(\mathbf{C_1}, \mathbf{Y_1}, \mathbf{p})$ and $(\mathbf{C_2}, \mathbf{Y_2}, \mathbf{q})$
with pairwise distance matrix $\mathbf{M}$ between node feature matrices $\mathbf{Y_1}$ and $\mathbf{Y_2}$,
estimated using Sinkhorn projections.

If `solver="PGD"`, the function solves the following entropic-regularized
Fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimization problem using Projected Gradient Descent \[12\]:

$$
\mathbf{T}^* \in \mathop{\arg\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \alpha \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l} - \epsilon H(\mathbf{T})

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Else if `solver="PPA"`, the function solves the following Fused Gromov-Wasserstein
optimization problem using Proximal Point Algorithm \[51\]:

$$
\mathbf{T}^* \in\mathop{\arg\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \alpha \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l}

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$: metric cost matrix between features across domains
- $\mathbf{C_1}$: Metric cost matrix in the source space
- $\mathbf{C_2}$: Metric cost matrix in the target space
- $\mathbf{p}$: distribution in the source space
- $\mathbf{q}$: distribution in the target space
- `L`: loss function to account for the misfit between the similarity and feature matrices
- `H`: entropy
- $\alpha$: trade-off parameter

:::{note}
If the inner solver `ot.sinkhorn` did not convergence, the
optimal coupling $\mathbf{T}$ returned by this function does not
necessarily satisfy the marginal constraints
$\mathbf{T}\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{p}$ and
$\mathbf{T}^T\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{q}$. So the returned
Fused Gromov-Wasserstein loss does not necessarily satisfy distance
properties and may be negative.
:::

# Parameters

M

: Metric cost matrix between features across domains

C1

: Metric cost matrix in the source space

C2

: Metric cost matrix in the target space

p

: Distribution in the source space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

q

: Distribution in the target space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either C1 and C2 are to be assumed symmetric or not.
  If let to its default None value, a symmetry test will be conducted.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

alpha

: Trade-off parameter (0 \< alpha \< 1)

G0: array-like, shape (ns,nt), optional

: If None the initial transport plan of the solver is pq^T.
  Otherwise G0 will be used as initial transport of the solver. G0 is not
  required to satisfy marginal constraints but we strongly recommend it
  to correctly estimate the GW distance.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

solver: string, optional

: Solver to use either 'PGD' for Projected Gradient Descent or 'PPA'
  for Proximal Point Algorithm.
  Default value is 'PGD'.

warmstart: bool, optional

: Either to perform warmstart of dual potentials in the successive
  Sinkhorn projections.

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: Record log if True.

kwargs: dict

: parameters can be directly passed to the ot.sinkhorn solver.
  Such as `numItermax` and `stopThr` to control its estimation precision,
  e.g \[51\] suggests to use `numItermax=1`.

# Returns

T

: Optimal coupling between the two joint spaces

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gabriel Peyré, Marco Cuturi, and Justin Solomon,
    "Gromov-Wasserstein averaging of kernel and distance matrices."
    International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML). 2016.

[^footnote-2]: Chowdhury, S., & Mémoli, F. (2019). The gromov–wasserstein
    distance between networks and stable network invariants.
    Information and Inference: A Journal of the IMA, 8(4), 757-787.

[^footnote-3]: Xu, H., Luo, D., Zha, H., & Duke, L. C. (2019). Gromov-wasserstein
    learning for graph matching and node embedding. In International
    Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2019.

[^footnote-4]: Vayer Titouan, Chapel Laetitia, Flamary Rémi, Tavenard Romain
    and Courty Nicolas "Optimal Transport for structured data with
    application on graphs", International Conference on Machine Learning
    (ICML). 2019.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.entropic_fused_gromov_wasserstein2
documentation:
Returns the Fused Gromov-Wasserstein distance between $(\mathbf{C_1}, \mathbf{Y_1}, \mathbf{p})$ and $(\mathbf{C_2}, \mathbf{Y_2}, \mathbf{q})$
with pairwise distance matrix $\mathbf{M}$ between node feature matrices $\mathbf{Y_1}$ and $\mathbf{Y_2}$,
estimated using Sinkhorn projections.

If `solver="PGD"`, the function solves the following entropic-regularized
Fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimization problem using Projected Gradient Descent \[12\]:

$$
\mathbf{FGW} = \mathop{\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \alpha \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l} - \epsilon H(\mathbf{T})

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Else if `solver="PPA"`, the function solves the following Fused Gromov-Wasserstein
optimization problem using Proximal Point Algorithm \[51\]:

$$
\mathbf{FGW} = \mathop{\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \alpha \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l}

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$: metric cost matrix between features across domains
- $\mathbf{C_1}$: Metric cost matrix in the source space
- $\mathbf{C_2}$: Metric cost matrix in the target space
- $\mathbf{p}$: distribution in the source space
- $\mathbf{q}$: distribution in the target space
- `L`: loss function to account for the misfit between the similarity and feature matrices
- `H`: entropy
- $\alpha$: trade-off parameter

:::{note}
If the inner solver `ot.sinkhorn` did not convergence, the
optimal coupling $\mathbf{T}$ returned by this function does not
necessarily satisfy the marginal constraints
$\mathbf{T}\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{p}$ and
$\mathbf{T}^T\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{q}$. So the returned
Fused Gromov-Wasserstein loss does not necessarily satisfy distance
properties and may be negative.
:::

# Parameters

M

: Metric cost matrix between features across domains

C1

: Metric cost matrix in the source space

C2

: Metric cost matrix in the target space

p

: Distribution in the source space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

q

: Distribution in the target space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either C1 and C2 are to be assumed symmetric or not.
  If let to its default None value, a symmetry test will be conducted.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

alpha

: Trade-off parameter (0 \< alpha \< 1)

G0: array-like, shape (ns,nt), optional

: If None the initial transport plan of the solver is pq^T.
  Otherwise G0 will be used as initial transport of the solver. G0 is not
  required to satisfy marginal constraints but we strongly recommend it
  to correctly estimate the GW distance.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: Record log if True.

# Returns

fgw_dist

: Fused Gromov-Wasserstein distance

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gabriel Peyré, Marco Cuturi, and Justin Solomon,
    "Gromov-Wasserstein averaging of kernel and distance matrices."
    International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML). 2016.

[^footnote-2]: Xu, H., Luo, D., Zha, H., & Duke, L. C. (2019). Gromov-wasserstein
    learning for graph matching and node embedding. In International
    Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2019.

[^footnote-3]: Vayer Titouan, Chapel Laetitia, Flamary Rémi, Tavenard Romain
    and Courty Nicolas "Optimal Transport for structured data with
    application on graphs", International Conference on Machine Learning
    (ICML). 2019.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.BAPG_fused_gromov_wasserstein
documentation:
Returns the Fused Gromov-Wasserstein transport between $(\mathbf{C_1}, \mathbf{Y_1}, \mathbf{p})$ and $(\mathbf{C_2}, \mathbf{Y_2}, \mathbf{q})$
with pairwise distance matrix $\mathbf{M}$ between node feature matrices $\mathbf{Y_1}$ and $\mathbf{Y_2}$,
estimated using Bregman Alternated Projected Gradient method.

If `marginal_loss=True`, the function solves the following Fused Gromov-Wasserstein
optimization problem :

$$
\mathbf{T}^* \in\mathop{\arg\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \alpha \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l}

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Else, the function solves an equivalent problem \[63, 64\], where constant terms only
depending on the marginals $\mathbf{p}$: and $\mathbf{q}$: are
discarded while assuming that L decomposes as in Proposition 1 in \[12\]:

$$
\mathbf{T}^* \in\mathop{\arg\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F - \alpha \langle h_1(\mathbf{C}_1) \mathbf{T} h_2(\mathbf{C_2})^\top , \mathbf{T} \rangle_F s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

\mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$: pairwise relation matrix between features across domains
- $\mathbf{C_1}$: Metric cost matrix in the source space
- $\mathbf{C_2}$: Metric cost matrix in the target space
- $\mathbf{p}$: distribution in the source space
- $\mathbf{q}$: distribution in the target space
- `L`: loss function to account for the misfit between the similarity and feature matrices
  : satisfying $L(a, b) = f_1(a) + f_2(b) - h_1(a) h_2(b)$
- $\alpha$: trade-off parameter

:::{note}
By algorithmic design the optimal coupling $\mathbf{T}$
returned by this function does not necessarily satisfy the marginal
constraints $\mathbf{T}\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{p}$ and
$\mathbf{T}^T\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{q}$. So the returned Fused
Gromov-Wasserstein loss does not necessarily satisfy distance
properties and may be negative.
:::

# Parameters

M

: Pairwise relation matrix between features across domains

C1

: Metric cost matrix in the source space

C2

: Metric cost matrix in the target space

p

: Distribution in the source space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

q

: Distribution in the target space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either C1 and C2 are to be assumed symmetric or not.
  If let to its default None value, a symmetry test will be conducted.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

alpha

: Trade-off parameter (0 \< alpha \< 1)

G0: array-like, shape (ns,nt), optional

: If None the initial transport plan of the solver is pq^T.
  Otherwise G0 will be used as initial transport of the solver. G0 is not
  required to satisfy marginal constraints but we strongly recommend it
  to correctly estimate the GW distance.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

marginal_loss: bool, optional. Default is False.

: Include constant marginal terms or not in the objective function.

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: Record log if True.

# Returns

T

: Optimal coupling between the two joint spaces

# References

[^footnote-1]: Li, J., Tang, J., Kong, L., Liu, H., Li, J., So, A. M. C., & Blanchet, J.
    "A Convergent Single-Loop Algorithm for Relaxation of Gromov-Wasserstein
    in Graph Data". International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2023.

[^footnote-2]: Ma, X., Chu, X., Wang, Y., Lin, Y., Zhao, J., Ma, L., & Zhu, W.
    "Fused Gromov-Wasserstein Graph Mixup for Graph-level Classifications".
    In Thirty-seventh Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.BAPG_fused_gromov_wasserstein2
documentation:
Returns the Fused Gromov-Wasserstein loss between $(\mathbf{C_1}, \mathbf{Y_1}, \mathbf{p})$ and $(\mathbf{C_2}, \mathbf{Y_2}, \mathbf{q})$
with pairwise distance matrix $\mathbf{M}$ between node feature matrices $\mathbf{Y_1}$ and $\mathbf{Y_2}$,
estimated using Bregman Alternated Projected Gradient method.

If `marginal_loss=True`, the function solves the following Fused Gromov-Wasserstein
optimization problem :

$$
\mathbf{FGW} = \mathop{\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \alpha \sum_{i,j,k,l} L(\mathbf{C_1}_{i,k}, \mathbf{C_2}_{j,l}) \mathbf{T}_{i,j} \mathbf{T}_{k,l}

s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

     \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Else, the function solves an equivalent problem \[63, 64\], where constant terms only
depending on the marginals $\mathbf{p}$: and $\mathbf{q}$: are
discarded while assuming that L decomposes as in Proposition 1 in \[12\]:

$$
\mathop{\min}_\mathbf{T} \quad (1 - \alpha) \langle \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F - \alpha \langle h_1(\mathbf{C}_1) \mathbf{T} h_2(\mathbf{C_2})^\top , \mathbf{T} \rangle_F s.t. \ \mathbf{T} \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{p}

\mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{1} &= \mathbf{q}

     \mathbf{T} &\geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$: metric cost matrix between features across domains
- $\mathbf{C_1}$: Metric cost matrix in the source space
- $\mathbf{C_2}$: Metric cost matrix in the target space
- $\mathbf{p}$: distribution in the source space
- $\mathbf{q}$: distribution in the target space
- `L`: loss function to account for the misfit between the similarity and feature matrices
  : satisfying $L(a, b) = f_1(a) + f_2(b) - h_1(a) h_2(b)$
- $\alpha$: trade-off parameter

:::{note}
By algorithmic design the optimal coupling $\mathbf{T}$
returned by this function does not necessarily satisfy the marginal
constraints $\mathbf{T}\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{p}$ and
$\mathbf{T}^T\mathbf{1}=\mathbf{q}$. So the returned Fused
Gromov-Wasserstein loss does not necessarily satisfy distance
properties and may be negative.
:::

# Parameters

M

: Metric cost matrix between features across domains

C1

: Metric cost matrix in the source space

C2

: Metric cost matrix in the target space

p

: Distribution in the source space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

q

: Distribution in the target space.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either C1 and C2 are to be assumed symmetric or not.
  If let to its default None value, a symmetry test will be conducted.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

alpha

: Trade-off parameter (0 \< alpha \< 1)

G0: array-like, shape (ns,nt), optional

: If None the initial transport plan of the solver is pq^T.
  Otherwise G0 will be used as initial transport of the solver. G0 is not
  required to satisfy marginal constraints but we strongly recommend it
  to correctly estimate the GW distance.

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

marginal_loss: bool, optional. Default is False.

: Include constant marginal terms or not in the objective function.

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: Record log if True.

# Returns

T

: Optimal coupling between the two joint spaces

# References

[^footnote-1]: Li, J., Tang, J., Kong, L., Liu, H., Li, J., So, A. M. C., & Blanchet, J.
    "A Convergent Single-Loop Algorithm for Relaxation of Gromov-Wasserstein
    in Graph Data". International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2023.

[^footnote-2]: Ma, X., Chu, X., Wang, Y., Lin, Y., Zhao, J., Ma, L., & Zhu, W.
    "Fused Gromov-Wasserstein Graph Mixup for Graph-level Classifications".
    In Thirty-seventh Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems.

functions.name: ot.gromov._bregman.entropic_fused_gromov_barycenters
documentation:
Returns the Fused Gromov-Wasserstein barycenters of `S` measurable networks with node features $(\mathbf{C}_s, \mathbf{Y}_s, \mathbf{p}_s)_{1 \leq s \leq S}$
estimated using Fused Gromov-Wasserstein transports from Sinkhorn projections.

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\mathbf{C}^*, \mathbf{Y}^* = \mathop{\arg \min}_{\mathbf{C}\in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}, \mathbf{Y}\in \mathbb{Y}^{N \times d}} \quad \sum_s \lambda_s \mathrm{FGW}_{\alpha}(\mathbf{C}, \mathbf{C}_s, \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{Y}_s, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{p}_s)
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{Y}_s$: feature matrix
- $\mathbf{C}_s$: metric cost matrix
- $\mathbf{p}_s$: distribution

# Parameters

N

: Size of the targeted barycenter

Ys: list of array-like, each element has shape (ns,d)

: Features of all samples

Cs

: Metric cost matrices

ps

: Sample weights in the `S` spaces.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distributions are taken.

p

: Weights in the targeted barycenter.
  If let to its default value None, uniform distribution is taken.

lambdas

: List of the `S` spaces' weights.
  If let to its default value None, uniform weights are taken.

loss_fun

: Loss function used for the solver either 'square_loss' or 'kl_loss'

epsilon

: Regularization term >0

symmetric

: Either structures are to be assumed symmetric or not. Default value is True.
  Else if set to True (resp. False), C1 and C2 will be assumed symmetric (resp. asymmetric).

alpha

: Trade-off parameter (0 \< alpha \< 1)

max_iter

: Max number of iterations

tol

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

stop_criterion

: Stop criterion taking values in \['barycenter', 'loss'\]. If set to 'barycenter'
  uses absolute norm variations of estimated barycenters. Else if set to 'loss'
  uses the relative variations of the loss.

warmstartT: bool, optional

: Either to perform warmstart of transport plans in the successive
  fused gromov-wasserstein transport problems.

verbose

: Print information along iterations.

log

: Record log if True.

init_C

: Random initial value for the $\mathbf{C}$ matrix provided by user.

init_Y

: Initialization for the barycenters' features. If not set a
  random init is used.

fixed_structure

: Whether to fix the structure of the barycenter during the updates.

fixed_features

: Whether to fix the feature of the barycenter during the updates

random_state

: Fix the seed for reproducibility

kwargs: dict

: parameters can be directly passed to the `ot.entropic_fused_gromov_wasserstein` solver.

# Returns

Y

: Feature matrix in the barycenter space (permutated arbitrarily)

C

: Similarity matrix in the barycenter space (permutated as Y's rows)

log

: Only returned when log=True. It contains the keys:

  - $\mathbf{T}$: list of (`N`, `ns`) transport matrices
  - $\mathbf{p}$: (`N`,) barycenter weights
  - $(\mathbf{M}_s)_s$: all distance matrices between the feature of the barycenter and the other features $(dist(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}_s))_s$ shape (`N`, `ns`)
  - values used in convergence evaluation.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gabriel Peyré, Marco Cuturi, and Justin Solomon,
    "Gromov-Wasserstein averaging of kernel and distance matrices."
    International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML). 2016.

[^footnote-2]: Vayer Titouan, Chapel Laetitia, Flamary Rémi, Tavenard Romain
    and Courty Nicolas
    "Optimal Transport for structured data with application on graphs"
    International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML). 2019.

modules.name: ot.stochastic
documentation: Stochastic solvers for regularized OT.

functions.name: ot.stochastic.coordinate_grad_semi_dual
documentation:
Compute the coordinate gradient update for regularized discrete distributions for $(i, :)$

The function computes the gradient of the semi dual problem:

$$
\max_\mathbf{v} \ \sum_i \mathbf{a}_i \left[ \sum_j \mathbf{v}_j \mathbf{b}_j - \mathrm{reg} \cdot \log \left( \sum_j \mathbf{b}_j \exp \left( \frac{\mathbf{v}_j - \mathbf{M}_{i,j}}{\mathrm{reg}} \right) \right) \right]
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\mathbf{v}$ is a dual variable in $\mathbb{R}^{nt}$
- reg is the regularization term
- $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are source and target weights (sum to 1)

The algorithm used for solving the problem is the ASGD & SAG algorithms
as proposed in {ref}`[18] <references-coordinate-grad-semi-dual>` \[alg.1 & alg.2\]

# Parameters

b

: Target measure.

M

: Cost matrix.

reg

: Regularization term > 0.

v

: Dual variable.

i

: Picked number `i`.

# Returns

coordinate gradient : ndarray, shape (nt,)

(references-coordinate-grad-semi-dual)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Genevay, A., Cuturi, M., Peyré, G. & Bach, F. (2016) Stochastic Optimization for Large-scale Optimal Transport. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (2016).
Optimization toolbox for SEMI - DUAL problems

functions.name: ot.stochastic.sag_entropic_transport
documentation:
Compute the SAG algorithm to solve the regularized discrete measures optimal transport max problem

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\gamma = \mathop{\arg \min}_\gamma \quad \langle \gamma, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \mathrm{reg} \cdot\Omega(\gamma)

s.t. \ \gamma \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{a}

     \gamma^T \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{b}

     \gamma \geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\Omega$ is the entropic regularization term with $\Omega(\gamma)=\sum_{i,j} \gamma_{i,j}\log(\gamma_{i,j})$
- $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are source and target weights (sum to 1)

The algorithm used for solving the problem is the SAG algorithm
as proposed in {ref}`[18] <references-sag-entropic-transport>` \[alg.1\]

# Parameters

a

: Source measure.

b

: Target measure.

M

: Cost matrix.

reg

: Regularization term > 0

numItermax

: Number of iteration.

lr

: Learning rate.

random_state

: Determines random number generation. Pass an int for reproducible
  output across multiple function calls.

# Returns

v

: Dual variable.

(references-sag-entropic-transport)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Genevay, A., Cuturi, M., Peyré, G. & Bach, F. (2016) Stochastic Optimization for Large-scale Optimal Transport. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (2016).

functions.name: ot.stochastic.averaged_sgd_entropic_transport
documentation:
Compute the ASGD algorithm to solve the regularized semi continuous measures optimal transport max problem

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\gamma = \mathop{\arg \min}_\gamma \quad \langle \gamma, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \mathrm{reg}\cdot\Omega(\gamma)

s.t. \gamma \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{a}

     \gamma^T \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{b}

     \gamma \geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\Omega$ is the entropic regularization term with $\Omega(\gamma)=\sum_{i,j} \gamma_{i,j}\log(\gamma_{i,j})$
- $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are source and target weights (sum to 1)

The algorithm used for solving the problem is the ASGD algorithm
as proposed in {ref}`[18] <references-averaged-sgd-entropic-transport>` \[alg.2\]

# Parameters

b

: target measure

M

: cost matrix

reg

: Regularization term > 0

numItermax

: Number of iteration.

lr

: Learning rate.

random_state

: Determines random number generation. Pass an int for reproducible
  output across multiple function calls.

# Returns

ave_v

: dual variable

(references-averaged-sgd-entropic-transport)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Genevay, A., Cuturi, M., Peyré, G. & Bach, F. (2016) Stochastic Optimization for Large-scale Optimal Transport. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (2016).

functions.name: ot.stochastic.c_transform_entropic
documentation:
The goal is to recover u from the c-transform.

The function computes the c-transform of a dual variable from the other
dual variable:

$$
\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{v}^{c,reg} = - \mathrm{reg} \sum_j \mathbf{b}_j \exp\left( \frac{\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{M}}{\mathrm{reg}} \right)
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{v}$ are dual variables in $\mathbb{R}^{ns} \times \mathbb{R}^{nt}$
- reg is the regularization term

It is used to recover an optimal u from optimal v solving the semi dual
problem, see Proposition 2.1 of {ref}`[18] <references-c-transform-entropic>`

# Parameters

b

: Target measure

M

: Cost matrix

reg

: Regularization term > 0

v

: Dual variable.

# Returns

u

: Dual variable.

(references-c-transform-entropic)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Genevay, A., Cuturi, M., Peyré, G. & Bach, F. (2016) Stochastic Optimization for Large-scale Optimal Transport. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (2016).

functions.name: ot.stochastic.solve_semi_dual_entropic
documentation:
Compute the transportation matrix to solve the regularized discrete measures optimal transport max problem

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\gamma = \mathop{\arg \min}_\gamma \quad \langle \gamma, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \mathrm{reg} \cdot\Omega(\gamma)

s.t. \ \gamma \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{a}

     \gamma^T \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{b}

     \gamma \geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\Omega$ is the entropic regularization term with $\Omega(\gamma)=\sum_{i,j} \gamma_{i,j}\log(\gamma_{i,j})$
- $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are source and target weights (sum to 1)

The algorithm used for solving the problem is the SAG or ASGD algorithms
as proposed in {ref}`[18] <references-solve-semi-dual-entropic>`

# Parameters

a

: source measure

b

: target measure

M

: cost matrix

reg

: Regularization term > 0

method

: used method (SAG or ASGD)

numItermax

: number of iteration

lr

: learning rate

n_source

: size of the source measure

n_target

: size of the target measure

log

: record log if True

# Returns

pi

: transportation matrix

log

: log dictionary return only if log==True in parameters

(references-solve-semi-dual-entropic)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Genevay, A., Cuturi, M., Peyré, G. & Bach, F. (2016) Stochastic Optimization for Large-scale Optimal Transport. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (2016).

functions.name: ot.stochastic.batch_grad_dual
documentation:
Computes the partial gradient of the dual optimal transport problem.

For each $(i,j)$ in a batch of coordinates, the partial gradients are :

$$
\partial_{\mathbf{u}_i} F = \frac{b_s}{l_v} \mathbf{u}_i - \sum_{j \in B_v} \mathbf{a}_i \mathbf{b}_j \exp\left( \frac{\mathbf{u}_i + \mathbf{v}_j - \mathbf{M}_{i,j}}{\mathrm{reg}} \right)

\partial_{\mathbf{v}_j} F = \frac{b_s}{l_u} \mathbf{v}_j -
\sum_{i \in B_u} \mathbf{a}_i \mathbf{b}_j
\exp\left( \frac{\mathbf{u}_i + \mathbf{v}_j - \mathbf{M}_{i,j}}{\mathrm{reg}} \right)
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{v}$ are dual variables in $\mathbb{R}^{ns} \times \mathbb{R}^{nt}$
- reg is the regularization term
- $B_u$ and $B_v$ are lists of index
- $b_s$ is the size of the batches $B_u$ and $B_v$
- $l_u$ and $l_v$ are the lengths of $B_u$ and $B_v$
- $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are source and target weights (sum to 1)

The algorithm used for solving the dual problem is the SGD algorithm
as proposed in {ref}`[19] <references-batch-grad-dual>` \[alg.1\]

# Parameters

a

: source measure

b

: target measure

M

: cost matrix

reg

: Regularization term > 0

alpha

: dual variable

beta

: dual variable

batch_size

: size of the batch

batch_alpha

: batch of index of alpha

batch_beta

: batch of index of beta

# Returns

grad

: partial grad F

(references-batch-grad-dual)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Seguy, V., Bhushan Damodaran, B., Flamary, R., Courty, N., Rolet, A.& Blondel, M. Large-scale Optimal Transport and Mapping Estimation. International Conference on Learning Representation (2018)
Optimization toolbox for DUAL problems

functions.name: ot.stochastic.sgd_entropic_regularization
documentation:
Compute the sgd algorithm to solve the regularized discrete measures optimal transport dual problem

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\gamma = \mathop{\arg \min}_\gamma \quad \langle \gamma, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \mathrm{reg} \cdot\Omega(\gamma)

s.t. \ \gamma \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{a}

     \gamma^T \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{b}

     \gamma \geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\Omega$ is the entropic regularization term with $\Omega(\gamma)=\sum_{i,j} \gamma_{i,j}\log(\gamma_{i,j})$
- $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are source and target weights (sum to 1)

# Parameters

a

: source measure

b

: target measure

M

: cost matrix

reg

: Regularization term > 0

batch_size

: size of the batch

numItermax

: number of iteration

lr

: learning rate

random_state

: Determines random number generation. Pass an int for reproducible
  output across multiple function calls.

# Returns

alpha

: dual variable

beta

: dual variable

# References

[^footnote-1]: Seguy, V., Bhushan Damodaran, B., Flamary, R., Courty, N., Rolet, A.& Blondel, M. Large-scale Optimal Transport and Mapping Estimation. International Conference on Learning Representation (2018)

functions.name: ot.stochastic.solve_dual_entropic
documentation:
Compute the transportation matrix to solve the regularized discrete measures optimal transport dual problem

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\gamma = \mathop{\arg \min}_\gamma \quad \langle \gamma, \mathbf{M} \rangle_F + \mathrm{reg} \cdot\Omega(\gamma)

s.t. \ \gamma \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{a}

     \gamma^T \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{b}

     \gamma \geq 0
$$

Where :

- $\mathbf{M}$ is the (`ns`, `nt`) metric cost matrix
- $\Omega$ is the entropic regularization term with $\Omega(\gamma)=\sum_{i,j} \gamma_{i,j}\log(\gamma_{i,j})$
- $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are source and target weights (sum to 1)

# Parameters

a

: source measure

b

: target measure

M

: cost matrix

reg

: Regularization term > 0

batch_size

: size of the batch

numItermax

: number of iteration

functions.name: ot.stochastic.loss_dual_entropic
documentation:
Compute the dual loss of the entropic OT as in equation (6)-(7) of \[19\]

This loss is backend compatible and can be used for stochastic optimization
of the dual potentials. It can be used on the full dataset (beware of
memory) or on minibatches.

# Parameters

u

: Source dual potential

v

: Target dual potential

xs

: Source samples

xt

: Target samples

reg

: Regularization term > 0 (default=1)

ws

: Source sample weights (default unif)

wt

: Target sample weights (default unif)

metric

: Ground metric for OT (default quadratic). Can be given as a callable
  function taking (xs,xt) as parameters.

# Returns

dual_loss

: Dual loss (to maximize)

# References

[^footnote-1]: Seguy, V., Bhushan Damodaran, B., Flamary, R., Courty, N., Rolet, A.& Blondel, M. Large-scale Optimal Transport and Mapping Estimation. International Conference on Learning Representation (2018)
Losses for stochastic optimization

modules.name: ot.gromov._unbalanced
documentation: Unbalanced Co-Optimal Transport and Fused Unbalanced Gromov-Wasserstein solvers
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: ot.gromov._unbalanced.fused_unbalanced_across_spaces_divergence
documentation:
Compute the fused unbalanced cross-spaces divergence between two matrices equipped
with the distributions on rows and columns. We consider two cases of matrix:

- (Squared) similarity matrix in Gromov-Wasserstein setting,

whose rows and columns represent the samples.

- Arbitrary-size matrix in Co-Optimal Transport setting,

whose rows represent samples, and columns represent corresponding features/dimensions.

More precisely, this function returns the sample and feature transport plans between
$(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{w}_{xs}, \mathbf{w}_{xf})$ and
$(\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{w}_{ys}, \mathbf{w}_{yf})$,
by solving the following problem using Block Coordinate Descent algorithm:

$$
\mathop{\arg \min}_{\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}}
&\quad \sum_{i,j,k,l}
(\mathbf{X}_{i,k} - \mathbf{Y}_{j,l})^2 \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} \\
&+ \rho_s \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 1} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 1}^T | \mathbf{w}_{xs} \mathbf{w}_{ys}^T)
+ \rho_f \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 2} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 2}^T | \mathbf{w}_{xf} \mathbf{w}_{yf}^T) \\
&+ \alpha_s \sum_{i,j} \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{M^{(s)}}_{i, j}
+ \alpha_f \sum_{k, l} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} \mathbf{M^{(f)}}_{k, l}
+ \mathbf{Reg}(\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q})
$$

Where:

- $\mathbf{X}$: Source input (arbitrary-size) matrix
- $\mathbf{Y}$: Target input (arbitrary-size) matrix
- $\mathbf{M^{(s)}}$: Additional sample matrix
- $\mathbf{M^{(f)}}$: Additional feature matrix
- $\mathbf{w}_{xs}$: Distribution of the samples in the source space
- $\mathbf{w}_{xf}$: Distribution of the features in the source space
- $\mathbf{w}_{ys}$: Distribution of the samples in the target space
- $\mathbf{w}_{yf}$: Distribution of the features in the target space
- $\mathbf{Div}$: Either Kullback-Leibler divergence or half-squared L2 norm.
- $\mathbf{Reg}$: Regularizer for sample and feature couplings.

We consider two types of regularizer:
: - Independent regularization used in unbalanced Co-Optimal Transport

  $$
  \mathbf{Reg}(\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}) = \varepsilon_s \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P} | \mathbf{w}_{xs} \mathbf{w}_{ys}^T) + \varepsilon_f \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{Q} | \mathbf{w}_{xf} \mathbf{w}_{yf}^T)
  $$

  - Joint regularization used in fused unbalanced Gromov-Wasserstein

  $$
  \mathbf{Reg}(\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}) = \varepsilon \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P} \otimes \mathbf{Q} | (\mathbf{w}_{xs} \mathbf{w}_{ys}^T) \otimes (\mathbf{w}_{xf} \mathbf{w}_{yf}^T) )
  $$

:::{note}
This function allows epsilon to be zero. In that case, `unbalanced_method` must be either "mm" or "lbfgsb".
:::

# Parameters

X

: Source input matrix.

Y

: Target input matrix.

wx_samp

: Histogram assigned on rows (samples) of matrix X.
  Uniform distribution by default.

wx_feat

: Histogram assigned on columns (features) of matrix X.
  Uniform distribution by default.

wy_samp

: Histogram assigned on rows (samples) of matrix Y.
  Uniform distribution by default.

wy_feat

: Histogram assigned on columns (features) of matrix Y.
  Uniform distribution by default.

reg_marginals: float or indexable object of length 1 or 2

: Marginal relaxation terms for sample and feature couplings.
  If `reg_marginals` is a scalar or an indexable object of length 1,
  then the same value is applied to both marginal relaxations.

epsilon

: Regularization parameters for entropic approximation of sample and feature couplings.
  Allow the case where `epsilon` contains 0. In that case, the MM solver is used by default
  instead of Sinkhorn solver. If `epsilon` is scalar, then the same value is applied to
  both regularization of sample and feature couplings.

reg_type: string, optional

> - If `reg_type` = "joint": then use joint regularization for couplings.
> - If `reg_type` = "independent": then use independent regularization for couplings.

divergence : string, optional (default = "kl")

> - If `divergence` = "kl", then Div is the Kullback-Leibler divergence.
> - If `divergence` = "l2", then Div is the half squared Euclidean norm.

unbalanced_solver

: Solver for the unbalanced OT subroutine.

  - If `divergence` = "kl", then `unbalanced_solver` can be: "sinkhorn", "sinkhorn_log", "mm", "lbfgsb"
  - If `divergence` = "l2", then `unbalanced_solver` can be "mm", "lbfgsb"

alpha

: Coeffficient parameter of linear terms with respect to the sample and feature couplings.
  If alpha is scalar, then the same alpha is applied to both linear terms.

M_samp

: Sample matrix associated to the Wasserstein linear term on sample coupling.

M_feat

: Feature matrix associated to the Wasserstein linear term on feature coupling.

rescale_plan

: If True, then rescale the sample and feature transport plans within each BCD iteration,
  so that they always have equal mass.

init_pi

: (n_feature_x, n_feature_y), optional (default = None).
  Initialization of sample and feature couplings.
  Uniform distributions by default.

init_duals

: Initialization of sample and feature dual vectors
  if using Sinkhorn algorithm. Zero vectors by default.

max_iter

: Number of Block Coordinate Descent (BCD) iterations.

tol

: Tolerance of BCD scheme. If the L1-norm between the current and previous
  sample couplings is under this threshold, then stop BCD scheme.

max_iter_ot

: Number of iterations to solve each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

tol_ot

: Tolerance of unbalanced solver for each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

log

: If True then the cost and four dual vectors, including
  two from sample and two from feature couplings, are recorded.

verbose

: If True then print the COOT cost at every multiplier of `eval_bcd`-th iteration.

# Returns

pi_samp

: Sample coupling matrix.

pi_feat

: Feature coupling matrix.

log

: Returned if `log` is True. The keys are:

  > error
  >
  > : list of L1 norms between the current and previous sample coupling.
  >
  > duals_sample
  >
  > : Pair of dual vectors when solving OT problem w.r.t the sample coupling.
  >
  > duals_feature
  >
  > : Pair of dual vectors when solving OT problem w.r.t the feature coupling.
  >
  > linear
  >
  > : Linear part of the cost.
  >
  > ucoot
  >
  > : Total cost.
  >
  > backend
  >
  > : The proper backend for all input arrays

functions.name: ot.gromov._unbalanced.unbalanced_co_optimal_transport
documentation:
Compute the unbalanced Co-Optimal Transport between two Euclidean point clouds
(represented as matrices whose rows are samples and columns are the features/dimensions).

More precisely, this function returns the sample and feature transport plans between
$(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{w}_{xs}, \mathbf{w}_{xf})$ and
$(\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{w}_{ys}, \mathbf{w}_{yf})$,
by solving the following problem using Block Coordinate Descent algorithm:

$$
\mathop{\arg \min}_{\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}} &\quad \sum_{i,j,k,l} (\mathbf{X}_{i,k} - \mathbf{Y}_{j,l})^2 \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} \\ &+ \rho_s \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 1} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 1}^T | \mathbf{w}_{xs} \mathbf{w}_{ys}^T) + \rho_f \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 2} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 2}^T | \mathbf{w}_{xf} \mathbf{w}_{yf}^T) \\ &+ \alpha_s \sum_{i,j} \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{M^{(s)}}_{i, j} + \alpha_f \sum_{k, l} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} \mathbf{M^{(f)}}_{k, l} \\ &+ \varepsilon_s \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P} | \mathbf{w}_{xs} \mathbf{w}_{ys}^T) + \varepsilon_f \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{Q} | \mathbf{w}_{xf} \mathbf{w}_{yf}^T)
$$

Where:

- $\mathbf{X}$: Source input (arbitrary-size) matrix
- $\mathbf{Y}$: Target input (arbitrary-size) matrix
- $\mathbf{M^{(s)}}$: Additional sample matrix
- $\mathbf{M^{(f)}}$: Additional feature matrix
- $\mathbf{w}_{xs}$: Distribution of the samples in the source space

- $\mathbf{w}_{ys}$: Distribution of the samples in the target space

- $\mathbf{Div}$: Either Kullback-Leibler divergence or half-squared L2 norm.

:::{note}
This function allows `epsilon` to be zero. In that case, `unbalanced_method` must be either "mm" or "lbfgsb".

epsilon

: Regularization parameters for entropic approximation of sample and feature couplings.
  Allow the case where `epsilon` contains 0. In that case, the MM solver is used by default
  instead of Sinkhorn solver. If `epsilon` is scalar, then the same value is applied to
  both regularization of sample and feature couplings.

divergence : string, optional (default = "kl")

> - If `divergence` = "kl", then Div is the Kullback-Leibler divergence.
> - If `divergence` = "l2", then Div is the half squared Euclidean norm.

unbalanced_solver

: Solver for the unbalanced OT subroutine.

  - If `divergence` = "kl", then `unbalanced_solver` can be: "sinkhorn", "sinkhorn_log", "mm", "lbfgsb"
  - If `divergence` = "l2", then `unbalanced_solver` can be "mm", "lbfgsb"

alpha

: Coeffficient parameter of linear terms with respect to the sample and feature couplings.
  If alpha is scalar, then the same alpha is applied to both linear terms.

M_samp

: Sample matrix associated to the Wasserstein linear term on sample coupling.

M_feat

: If True, then rescale the sample and feature transport plans within each BCD iteration,

functions.name: ot.gromov._unbalanced.unbalanced_co_optimal_transport2
documentation:
Compute the unbalanced Co-Optimal Transport between two Euclidean point clouds
(represented as matrices whose rows are samples and columns are the features/dimensions).

More precisely, this function returns the unbalanced Co-Optimal Transport cost between
$(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{w}_{xs}, \mathbf{w}_{xf})$ and
$(\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{w}_{ys}, \mathbf{w}_{yf})$,
by solving the following problem using Block Coordinate Descent algorithm:

$$
\mathop{\min}_{\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}} &\quad \sum_{i,j,k,l} (\mathbf{X}_{i,k} - \mathbf{Y}_{j,l})^2 \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} \\ &+ \rho_s \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 1} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 1}^T | \mathbf{w}_{xs} \mathbf{w}_{ys}^T) + \rho_f \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 2} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 2}^T | \mathbf{w}_{xf} \mathbf{w}_{yf}^T) \\ &+ \alpha_s \sum_{i,j} \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{M^{(s)}}_{i, j} + \alpha_f \sum_{k, l} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} \mathbf{M^{(f)}}_{k, l} \\ &+ \varepsilon_s \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P} | \mathbf{w}_{xs} \mathbf{w}_{ys}^T) + \varepsilon_f \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{Q} | \mathbf{w}_{xf} \mathbf{w}_{yf}^T)
$$

Where:

- $\mathbf{X}$: Source input (arbitrary-size) matrix
- $\mathbf{Y}$: Target input (arbitrary-size) matrix

- $\mathbf{M^{(f)}}$: Additional feature matrix

:::{note}
This function allows `epsilon` to be zero. In that case, `unbalanced_method` must be either "mm" or "lbfgsb".
Also the computation of gradients is only supported for KL divergence. The case of half squared-L2 norm uses those of KL divergence.
:::

# Parameters

reg_marginals: float or indexable object of length 1 or 2

: Marginal relaxation terms for sample and feature couplings.
  If `reg_marginals` is a scalar or an indexable object of length 1,
  then the same value is applied to both marginal relaxations.

epsilon

: Regularization parameters for entropic approximation of sample and feature couplings.
  Allow the case where `epsilon` contains 0. In that case, the MM solver is used by default
  instead of Sinkhorn solver. If `epsilon` is scalar, then the same value is applied to
  both regularization of sample and feature couplings.

divergence : string, optional (default = "kl")

> - If `divergence` = "kl", then Div is the Kullback-Leibler divergence.
> - If `divergence` = "l2", then Div is the half squared Euclidean norm.

unbalanced_solver

: Solver for the unbalanced OT subroutine.

  - If `divergence` = "kl", then `unbalanced_solver` can be: "sinkhorn", "sinkhorn_log", "mm", "lbfgsb"
  - If `divergence` = "l2", then `unbalanced_solver` can be "mm", "lbfgsb"

alpha

: Coeffficient parameter of linear terms with respect to the sample and feature couplings.
  If alpha is scalar, then the same alpha is applied to both linear terms.

M_samp

: Sample matrix associated to the Wasserstein linear term on sample coupling.

M_feat

: Feature matrix associated to the Wasserstein linear term on feature coupling.

rescale_plan

: If True, then rescale the transport plans in each BCD iteration,
  so that they always have equal mass.

init_pi

: (n_feature_x, n_feature_y), optional (default = None).
  Initialization of sample and feature couplings.
  Uniform distributions by default.

init_duals

: Initialization of sample and feature dual vectors
  if using Sinkhorn algorithm. Zero vectors by default.

max_iter

: Number of Block Coordinate Descent (BCD) iterations.

tol

: Tolerance of BCD scheme. If the L1-norm between the current and previous
  sample couplings is under this threshold, then stop BCD scheme.

max_iter_ot

: Number of iterations to solve each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

tol_ot

: Tolerance of unbalanced solver for each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

log

: If True then the cost and four dual vectors, including
  two from sample and two from feature couplings, are recorded.

verbose

: If True then print the COOT cost at every multiplier of `eval_bcd`-th iteration.

# Returns

ucoot

: UCOOT cost.

log

: Returned if `log` is True. The keys are:

  > error
  >
  > : list of L1 norms between the current and previous sample coupling.
  >
  > duals_sample
  >
  > : Pair of dual vectors when solving OT problem w.r.t the sample coupling.
  >
  > duals_feature
  >
  > : Pair of dual vectors when solving OT problem w.r.t the feature coupling.
  >
  > linear
  >
  > : Linear part of UCOOT cost.
  >
  > ucoot
  >
  > : UCOOT cost.
  >
  > backend
  >
  > : The proper backend for all input arrays

# References

[^footnote-1]: Tran, H., Janati, H., Courty, N., Flamary, R., Redko, I., Demetci, P., & Singh, R.
    Unbalanced Co-Optimal Transport. AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 2023.

functions.name: ot.gromov._unbalanced.fused_unbalanced_gromov_wasserstein
documentation:
Compute the lower bound of the fused unbalanced Gromov-Wasserstein (FUGW) between two similarity matrices.
In practice, this lower bound is used interchangeably with the true FUGW.

More precisely, this function returns the transport plan between
$(\mathbf{C^X}, \mathbf{w_X})$ and $(\mathbf{C^Y}, \mathbf{w_Y})$,
by solving the following problem using Block Coordinate Descent algorithm:

$$
\mathop{\arg \min}_{\substack{\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}: \\ mass(P) = mass(Q)}} &\quad \sum_{i,j,k,l} (\mathbf{C^X}_{i,k} - \mathbf{C^Y}_{j,l})^2 \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} + \frac{\alpha}{2} \sum_{i,j} (\mathbf{P}_{i,j} + \mathbf{Q}_{i,j}) \mathbf{M}_{i, j} \\ &+ \rho_1 \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 1} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 1}^T | \mathbf{w_X} \mathbf{w_X}^T) + \rho_2 \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 2} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 2}^T | \mathbf{w_Y} \mathbf{w_Y}^T) \\ &+ \varepsilon \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P} \otimes \mathbf{Q} | (\mathbf{w_X} \mathbf{w_Y}^T) \otimes (\mathbf{w_X} \mathbf{w_Y}^T))
$$

Where:

- $\mathbf{C^X}$: Source similarity matrix
- $\mathbf{C^Y}$: Target similarity matrix
- $\mathbf{M}$: Sample matrix corresponding to the Wasserstein term
- $\mathbf{w_X}$: Distribution of the samples in the source space
- $\mathbf{w_Y}$: Distribution of the samples in the target space
- $\mathbf{Div}$: Either Kullback-Leibler divergence or half-squared L2 norm.

:::{note}
This function allows epsilon to be zero. In that case, `unbalanced_method` must be either "mm" or "lbfgsb".
:::

# Parameters

Cx

: Source similarity matrix.

Cy

: Target similarity matrix.

wx

: Histogram assigned on rows (samples) of matrix Cx.
  Uniform distribution by default.

wy

: Histogram assigned on rows (samples) of matrix Cy.
  Uniform distribution by default.

reg_marginals: float or indexable object of length 1 or 2

: Marginal relaxation terms for sample and feature couplings.
  If `reg_marginals` is a scalar or an indexable object of length 1,
  then the same value is applied to both marginal relaxations.

epsilon

: Regularization parameters for entropic approximation of sample and feature couplings.
  Allow the case where `epsilon` contains 0. In that case, the MM solver is used by default
  instead of Sinkhorn solver. If `epsilon` is scalar, then the same value is applied to
  both regularization of sample and feature couplings.

divergence : string, optional (default = "kl")

> - If `divergence` = "kl", then Div is the Kullback-Leibler divergence.
> - If `divergence` = "l2", then Div is the half squared Euclidean norm.

unbalanced_solver

: Solver for the unbalanced OT subroutine.

  - If `divergence` = "kl", then `unbalanced_solver` can be: "sinkhorn", "sinkhorn_log", "mm", "lbfgsb"
  - If `divergence` = "l2", then `unbalanced_solver` can be "mm", "lbfgsb"

alpha

: Coeffficient parameter of linear terms with respect to the sample and feature couplings.
  If alpha is scalar, then the same alpha is applied to both linear terms.

M

: Sample matrix associated to the Wasserstein linear term on sample coupling.

init_pi :(n_sample_x, n_sample_y) array-like, optional (default = None)

: Initialization of sample coupling. By default = $w_X w_Y^T$.

init_duals

: Initialization of sample and feature dual vectors
  if using Sinkhorn algorithm. Zero vectors by default.

max_iter

: Number of Block Coordinate Descent (BCD) iterations.

tol

: Tolerance of BCD scheme. If the L1-norm between the current and previous
  sample couplings is under this threshold, then stop BCD scheme.

max_iter_ot

: Number of iterations to solve each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

tol_ot

: Tolerance of unbalanced solver for each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

log

: If True then the cost and four dual vectors, including
  two from sample and two from feature couplings, are recorded.

verbose

: If True then print the COOT cost at every multiplier of `eval_bcd`-th iteration.

# Returns

pi_samp

: Sample coupling matrix.
  In practice, we use this matrix as solution of FUGW.

pi_samp2

: Second sample coupling matrix.
  In practice, we usually ignore this output.

log

: Returned if `log` is True. The keys are:

  > error
  >
  > : list of L1 norms between the current and previous sample couplings.
  >
  > duals
  >
  > : Pair of dual vectors when solving OT problem w.r.t the sample coupling.
  >
  > linear
  >
  > : Linear part of FUGW cost.
  >
  > fugw_cost
  >
  > : Total FUGW cost.
  >
  > backend
  >
  > : The proper backend for all input arrays

# References

[^footnote-1]: Thual, A., Tran, H., Zemskova, T., Courty, N., Flamary, R., Dehaene, S., & Thirion, B.
    Aligning individual brains with Fused Unbalanced Gromov-Wasserstein.
    Advances in Neural Information Systems, 35 (2022).

[^footnote-2]: Thibault Séjourné, François-Xavier Vialard, & Gabriel Peyré.
    The Unbalanced Gromov Wasserstein Distance: Conic Formulation and Relaxation.
    Neural Information Processing Systems, 34 (2021).

functions.name: ot.gromov._unbalanced.fused_unbalanced_gromov_wasserstein2
documentation:
Compute the lower bound of the fused unbalanced Gromov-Wasserstein (FUGW) between two similarity matrices.
In practice, this lower bound is used interchangeably with the true FUGW.

More precisely, this function returns the lower bound of the fused unbalanced Gromov-Wasserstein cost between
$(\mathbf{C^X}, \mathbf{w_X})$ and $(\mathbf{C^Y}, \mathbf{w_Y})$,
by solving the following problem using Block Coordinate Descent algorithm:

$$
\mathop{\min}_{\substack{\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}: \\ mass(P) = mass(Q)}} &\quad \sum_{i,j,k,l} (\mathbf{C^X}_{i,k} - \mathbf{C^Y}_{j,l})^2 \mathbf{P}_{i,j} \mathbf{Q}_{k,l} + \frac{\alpha}{2} \sum_{i,j} (\mathbf{P}_{i,j} + \mathbf{Q}_{i,j}) \mathbf{M}_{i, j} \\ &+ \rho_1 \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 1} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 1}^T | \mathbf{w_X} \mathbf{w_X}^T) + \rho_2 \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P}_{\# 2} \mathbf{Q}_{\# 2}^T | \mathbf{w_Y} \mathbf{w_Y}^T) \\ &+ \varepsilon \mathbf{Div}(\mathbf{P} \otimes \mathbf{Q} | (\mathbf{w_X} \mathbf{w_Y}^T) \otimes (\mathbf{w_X} \mathbf{w_Y}^T))
$$

Where:

- $\mathbf{C^X}$: Source similarity matrix
- $\mathbf{C^Y}$: Target similarity matrix
- $\mathbf{M}$: Sample matrix corresponding to the Wasserstein term
- $\mathbf{w_X}$: Distribution of the samples in the source space
- $\mathbf{w_Y}$: Distribution of the samples in the target space
- $\mathbf{Div}$: Either Kullback-Leibler divergence or half-squared L2 norm.

:::{note}
This function allows `epsilon` to be zero. In that case, unbalanced_method must be either "mm" or "lbfgsb".
Also the computation of gradients is only supported for KL divergence, but not for half squared-L2 norm. In case of half squared-L2 norm, the calculation of KL divergence will be used.
:::

# Parameters

Cx

: Source similarity matrix.

Cy

: Target similarity matrix.

wx

: Histogram assigned on rows (samples) of matrix Cx.
  Uniform distribution by default.

wy

: Histogram assigned on rows (samples) of matrix Cy.
  Uniform distribution by default.

reg_marginals: float or indexable object of length 1 or 2

: Marginal relaxation terms for sample and feature couplings.
  If `reg_marginals` is a scalar or an indexable object of length 1,
  then the same value is applied to both marginal relaxations.

epsilon

: Regularization parameters for entropic approximation of sample and feature couplings.
  Allow the case where `epsilon` contains 0. In that case, the MM solver is used by default
  instead of Sinkhorn solver. If `epsilon` is scalar, then the same value is applied to
  both regularization of sample and feature couplings.

divergence : string, optional (default = "kl")

> - If `divergence` = "kl", then Div is the Kullback-Leibler divergence.
> - If `divergence` = "l2", then Div is the half squared Euclidean norm.

unbalanced_solver

: Solver for the unbalanced OT subroutine.

  - If `divergence` = "kl", then `unbalanced_solver` can be: "sinkhorn", "sinkhorn_log", "mm", "lbfgsb"
  - If `divergence` = "l2", then `unbalanced_solver` can be "mm", "lbfgsb"

alpha

: Coeffficient parameter of linear terms with respect to the sample and feature couplings.
  If alpha is scalar, then the same alpha is applied to both linear terms.

M

: Sample matrix associated to the Wasserstein linear term on sample coupling.

init_pi :(n_sample_x, n_sample_y) array-like, optional (default = None)

: Initialization of sample coupling. By default = $w_X w_Y^T$.

init_duals

: Initialization of sample and feature dual vectors
  if using Sinkhorn algorithm. Zero vectors by default.

max_iter

: Number of Block Coordinate Descent (BCD) iterations.

tol

: Tolerance of BCD scheme. If the L1-norm between the current and previous
  sample couplings is under this threshold, then stop BCD scheme.

max_iter_ot

: Number of iterations to solve each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

tol_ot

: Tolerance of unbalanced solver for each of the
  two unbalanced optimal transport problems in each BCD iteration.

log

: If True then the cost and four dual vectors, including
  two from sample and two from feature couplings, are recorded.

verbose

: If True then print the COOT cost at every multiplier of `eval_bcd`-th iteration.

# Returns

fugw

: Total FUGW cost

log

: Returned if `log` is True. The keys are:

  > error
  >
  > : list of L1 norms between the current and previous sample couplings.
  >
  > duals
  >
  > : Pair of dual vectors when solving OT problem w.r.t the sample coupling.
  >
  > linear
  >
  > : Linear part of FUGW cost.
  >
  > fugw_cost
  >
  > : Total FUGW cost.
  >
  > backend
  >
  > : The proper backend for all input arrays

# References

[^footnote-1]: Thual, A., Tran, H., Zemskova, T., Courty, N., Flamary, R., Dehaene, S., & Thirion, B.
    Aligning individual brains with Fused Unbalanced Gromov-Wasserstein.
    Advances in Neural Information Systems, 35 (2022).

[^footnote-2]: Thibault Séjourné, François-Xavier Vialard, & Gabriel Peyré.
    The Unbalanced Gromov Wasserstein Distance: Conic Formulation and Relaxation.
    Neural Information Processing Systems, 34 (2021).

modules.name: ot.plot
documentation:
Functions for plotting OT matrices

:::{warning}
Note that by default the module is not import in {mod}`ot`. In order to
use it you need to explicitly import {mod}`ot.plot`
:::

functions.name: ot.plot.plot1D_mat
documentation:
Plot matrix $\mathbf{M}$ with the source and target 1D distributions.

Creates a subplot with the source distribution $\mathbf{a}$ and target
distribution $\mathbf{b}`t.
In 'yx' mode (default), the source is on the left and
the target on the top, and in 'xy' mode, source on the bottom (upside
down) and the target on the left.
The matrix :math:$mathbf\{M}\` is shown in between.

# Parameters

a

: Source distribution

b

: Target distribution

M

: Matrix to plot

title

: Title of the plot

plot_style

: Style of the plot, 'yx' or 'xy'. 'yx' places the source on the left and
  the target on the top, 'xy' places the source on the bottom (upside
  down) and the target on the left.

a_label

: Label for source distribution

b_label

: Label for target distribution

color_source

: Color of the source distribution

color_target

: Color of the target distribution

coupling_cmap

: Colormap for the coupling matrix

# Returns

ax1 : source plot ax
ax2 : target plot ax
ax3 : coupling plot ax

:::{seealso}
{func}`rescale_for_imshow_plot`
:::

functions.name: ot.plot.rescale_for_imshow_plot
documentation:
Gives arrays xr, yr that can be plotted over an (n, m)
imshow plot (in 'xy' coordinates). If `a_y` or `b_y` is provided,
y is sliced over its indices such that y stays in \[ay, by\].

# Parameters

x : ndarray, shape (nx,)
y : ndarray, shape (nx,)
n : int

> x-axis size of the imshow plot on which to plot (x, y)

m

: y-axis size of the imshow plot, defaults to n

a_y

: Lower bound for y

b_y

: Upper bound for y

# Returns

xr

: Rescaled x values (due to slicing, may have less elements than x)

yr

: Rescaled y values (due to slicing, may have less elements than y)

:::{seealso}
{func}`plot1D_mat`
:::

functions.name: ot.plot.plot2D_samples_mat
documentation:
Plot matrix $\mathbf{G}$ in 2D with lines using alpha values

Plot lines between source and target 2D samples with a color
proportional to the value of the matrix $\mathbf{G}$ between samples.

# Parameters

xs

: Source samples positions

b

: Target samples positions

G

: OT matrix

thr

: threshold above which the line is drawn

kwargs

: parameters given to the plot functions (default color is black if
  nothing given)

Dimension reduction with OT

use it you need to explicitly import {mod}`ot.dr`
:::
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: ot.dr.dist
documentation: Compute squared euclidean distance between samples (autograd)

functions.name: ot.dr.sinkhorn
documentation: Sinkhorn algorithm with fixed number of iteration (autograd)

functions.name: ot.dr.logsumexp
documentation: Log-sum-exp reduction compatible with autograd (no numpy implementation)

functions.name: ot.dr.sinkhorn_log
documentation: Sinkhorn algorithm in log-domain with fixed number of iteration (autograd)

documentation: split samples in $\mathbf{X}$ by classes in $\mathbf{y}$

functions.name: ot.dr.fda
documentation:
Fisher Discriminant Analysis

Parameters
----------
X : ndarray, shape (n, d)
    Training samples.
y : ndarray, shape (n,)
    Labels for training samples.
p : int, optional
    Size of dimensionality reduction.
reg : float, optional
    Regularization term >0 (ridge regularization)

Returns
-------
P : ndarray, shape (d, p)
    Optimal transportation matrix for the given parameters
proj : callable
    projection function including mean centering

functions.name: ot.dr.wda
documentation:
Wasserstein Discriminant Analysis {ref}`[11] <references-wda>`

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\mathbf{P} = \mathop{\arg \min}_\mathbf{P} \quad \frac{\sum\limits_i W(P \mathbf{X}^i, P \mathbf{X}^i)}{\sum\limits_{i, j \neq i} W(P \mathbf{X}^i, P \mathbf{X}^j)}
$$

where :

- $P$ is a linear projection operator in the Stiefel(`p`, `d`) manifold
- $W$ is entropic regularized Wasserstein distances
- $\mathbf{X}^i$ are samples in the dataset corresponding to class i

**Choosing a Sinkhorn solver**

By default and when using a regularization parameter that is not too small
the default sinkhorn solver should be enough. If you need to use a small
regularization to get sparse cost matrices, you should use the
{py:func}`ot.dr.sinkhorn_log` solver that will avoid numerical
errors, but can be slow in practice.

# Parameters

X

: Training samples.

y

: Labels for training samples.

p

: Size of dimensionality reduction.

reg

: Regularization term >0 (entropic regularization)

solver

: None for steepest descent or 'TrustRegions' for trust regions algorithm
  else should be a pymanopt.solvers

sinkhorn_method

: method used for the Sinkhorn solver, either 'sinkhorn' or 'sinkhorn_log'

P0

: Initial starting point for projection.

normalize

: Normalize the Wasserstaiun distance by the average distance on P0 (default : False)

verbose

: Print information along iterations.

# Returns

P

: Optimal transportation matrix for the given parameters

proj

: Projection function including mean centering.

(references-wda)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Flamary, R., Cuturi, M., Courty, N., & Rakotomamonjy, A. (2016).
    Wasserstein Discriminant Analysis. arXiv preprint arXiv:1608.08063.

functions.name: ot.dr.projection_robust_wasserstein
documentation:
Projection Robust Wasserstein Distance {ref}`[32] <references-projection-robust-wasserstein>`

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\max_{U \in St(d, k)} \ \min_{\pi \in \Pi(\mu,\nu)} \quad \sum_{i,j} \pi_{i,j} \|U^T(\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)\|^2 - \mathrm{reg} \cdot H(\pi)
$$

- $U$ is a linear projection operator in the Stiefel(`d`, `k`) manifold
- $H(\pi)$ is entropy regularizer
- $\mathbf{x}_i$, $\mathbf{y}_j$ are samples of measures $\mu$ and $\nu$ respectively

# Parameters

X

: Samples from measure $\mu$

Y

: Samples from measure $\nu$

a

: weights for measure $\mu$

b

: weights for measure $\nu$

tau

: stepsize for Riemannian Gradient Descent

U0

: Initial starting point for projection.

reg

: Regularization term >0 (entropic regularization)

k

: Subspace dimension

stopThr

: Stop threshold on error (>0)

verbose

: Print information along iterations.

random_state

: Determines random number generation for initial value of projection
  operator when U0 is not given.

# Returns

pi

: Optimal transportation matrix for the given parameters

U

: Projection operator.

(references-projection-robust-wasserstein)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Huang, M. , Ma S. & Lai L. (2021).
    A Riemannian Block Coordinate Descent Method for Computing
    the Projection Robust Wasserstein Distance, ICML.

functions.name: ot.dr.ewca
documentation:
Entropic Wasserstein Component Analysis {ref}`[52] <references-entropic-wasserstein-component_analysis>`.

The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\mathbf{U} = \mathop{\arg \min}_\mathbf{U} \quad W(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{U}\mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{X})
$$

where :

- $\mathbf{U}$ is a matrix in the Stiefel(`p`, `d`) manifold
- $W$ is entropic regularized Wasserstein distances
- $\mathbf{X}$ are samples

# Parameters

X

: Samples from measure $\mu$.

U0

: Initial starting point for projection.

reg

: Regularization term >0 (entropic regularization).

k

: Subspace dimension.

method

: Eather 'BCD' or 'MM' (Block Coordinate Descent or Majorization-Minimization).
  Prefer MM when d is large.

sinkhorn_method

: Method used for the Sinkhorn solver, see {ref}`ot.bregman.sinkhorn` for more details.

stopThr

: Stop threshold on error (>0).

maxiter

: Maximum number of iterations of the BCD/MM.

maxiter_sink

: Maximum number of iterations of the Sinkhorn solver.

maxiter_MM

: Maximum number of iterations of the MM (only used when method='MM').

verbose

: Print information along iterations.

# Returns

pi

: Optimal transportation matrix for the given parameters.

U

: Matrix Stiefel manifold.

(references-entropic-wasserstein-component-analysis)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Collas, A., Vayer, T., Flamary, F., & Breloy, A. (2023).
    Entropic Wasserstein Component Analysis.

documentation: Bregman projections solvers for entropic regularized Wasserstein convolutional barycenters
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: ot.bregman._convolutional.convolutional_barycenter2d
documentation:
Compute the entropic regularized wasserstein barycenter of distributions $\mathbf{A}$
where $\mathbf{A}$ is a collection of 2D images.

> The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\mathbf{a} = \mathop{\arg \min}_\mathbf{a} \quad \sum_i W_{reg}(\mathbf{a},\mathbf{a}_i)
$$

where :

- $W_{reg}(\cdot,\cdot)$ is the entropic regularized Wasserstein
  distance (see {py:func}`ot.bregman.sinkhorn`)
- $\mathbf{a}_i$ are training distributions (2D images) in the mast two dimensions
  of matrix $\mathbf{A}$
- `reg` is the regularization strength scalar value

The algorithm used for solving the problem is the Sinkhorn-Knopp matrix scaling algorithm
as proposed in {ref}`[21] <references-convolutional-barycenter-2d>`

# Parameters

A

: `n` distributions (2D images) of size `width` x `height`

reg

: Regularization term >0

weights

: Weights of each image on the simplex (barycentric coordinates)

method

: method used for the solver either 'sinkhorn' or 'sinkhorn_log'

numItermax

: Max number of iterations

stopThr

: Stop threshold on error (> 0)

stabThr

: Stabilization threshold to avoid numerical precision issue

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: record log if True

warn

: if True, raises a warning if the algorithm doesn't convergence.

# Returns

a

: 2D Wasserstein barycenter

log

: log dictionary return only if log==True in parameters

(references-convolutional-barycenter-2d)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Solomon, J., De Goes, F., Peyré, G., Cuturi, M., Butscher,
    A., Nguyen, A. & Guibas, L. (2015).     Convolutional wasserstein distances:
    Efficient optimal transportation on geometric domains. ACM Transactions
    on Graphics (TOG), 34(4), 66

[^footnote-2]: Janati, H., Cuturi, M., Gramfort, A. Proceedings of the 37th
    International Conference on Machine Learning, PMLR 119:4692-4701, 2020

functions.name: ot.bregman._convolutional._convolutional_barycenter2d
documentation:
Compute the entropic regularized wasserstein barycenter of distributions A
where A is a collection of 2D images.

functions.name: ot.bregman._convolutional._convolutional_barycenter2d_log
documentation:
Compute the entropic regularized wasserstein barycenter of distributions A
where A is a collection of 2D images in log-domain.

functions.name: ot.bregman._convolutional.convolutional_barycenter2d_debiased
documentation:
Compute the debiased sinkhorn barycenter of distributions $\mathbf{A}$
where $\mathbf{A}$ is a collection of 2D images.

> The function solves the following optimization problem:

$$
\mathbf{a} = \mathop{\arg \min}_\mathbf{a} \quad \sum_i S_{reg}(\mathbf{a},\mathbf{a}_i)
$$

where :

- $S_{reg}(\cdot,\cdot)$ is the debiased entropic regularized Wasserstein
  distance (see {py:func}`ot.bregman.barycenter_debiased`)
- $\mathbf{a}_i$ are training distributions (2D images) in the mast two
  dimensions of matrix $\mathbf{A}$
- `reg` is the regularization strength scalar value

The algorithm used for solving the problem is the debiased Sinkhorn scaling
algorithm as proposed in {ref}`[37] <references-convolutional-barycenter2d-debiased>`

# Parameters

A

: `n` distributions (2D images) of size `width` x `height`

reg

: Regularization term >0

weights

: Weights of each image on the simplex (barycentric coordinates)

method

: method used for the solver either 'sinkhorn' or 'sinkhorn_log'

numItermax

: Max number of iterations

stopThr

: Stop threshold on error (> 0)

stabThr

: Stabilization threshold to avoid numerical precision issue

verbose

: Print information along iterations

log

: record log if True

warn

: if True, raises a warning if the algorithm doesn't convergence.

# Returns

a

: 2D Wasserstein barycenter

log

: log dictionary return only if log==True in parameters

(references-convolutional-barycenter2d-debiased)=

# References

[^footnote-1]: Janati, H., Cuturi, M., Gramfort, A. Proceedings of the 37th International
    Conference on Machine Learning, PMLR 119:4692-4701, 2020

functions.name: ot.bregman._convolutional._convolutional_barycenter2d_debiased
documentation: Compute the debiased barycenter of 2D images via sinkhorn convolutions.
